US and Iranian forces exchanged fire in the Strait of Hormuz, with the US military saying it intercepted "unprovoked Iranian attacks" and responded with "self-defence strikes" as three guided-missile destroyers transited the waterway.
Indonesia's Mount Dukono volcano erupted, killing three hikers including two foreigners and one local resident. The volcano, located on Halmahera island, sent huge plumes of ash into the sky.

Sean Irving argues that economic republicanism—19th-century heirs to Machiavelli and Jefferson—offers a forgotten blueprint for freedom today.

Flora Champy revisits Marcel Proust’s 1905 essay "On Reading" to defend literature as a technology of freedom rather than moral improvement. Proust rejects Ruskin’s utilitarian claim that books make us better;

Denva Gallant examines 13th-century mosaics in Venice’s San Marco to show how medieval art turned the body into a political text. In the Zen Chapel vault, Saint Mark is dragged by executioners yet remains impassive;

Tim Judah reports from Kyiv on Ukraine’s third winter of full-scale war. Rolling blackouts and nightly Russian drone strikes have turned survival into a civic routine: cafes brew coffee on propane, theaters rehearse by candlelight, and families time their showers to the thud of distant explosions.
Maria Popova distills Oliver Sacks’s posthumous essay collection to a single insight: perception is not a passive window but a creative mirror.

triadic_ontology.findings: Two new works deepen the triadic-ontology program. Damiano Migliorini’s monograph “Relational Ontologies and Trinitarian Metaphysics: Subsistences, Events and Gunk” (Academia.edu, 2025) proposes a speculative model that weds analytic philosophy of religion to quantum-structural realism, arguing that divine relationality escapes both monism and classical substance metaphysics while preserving rational coherence. Julien Tricard’s March 2025 Acta Analytica paper “An Ultimate Argument Against Nominalistic Relationalism” offers a parallel critique: by showing that purely relational accounts of quantity cannot fix determinate values, Tricard clears ground for a richer, triadic metaphysics in which relations and relata co-constitute reality. Finally, the Karl Alber series “Studies on Triadic Ontology and Trinitarian Philosophy” launches with the 2025 volume Trinitarian Ontologies, collecting twenty essays from Münster and Prague that trace the movement from patristic sources through quantum-t [TRUNCATED]

ai_systems.findings: Three new papers define the 2026 autonomous-research stack. DOVA introduces deliberation-first orchestration: before any tool is invoked, a meta-reasoning layer deliberates using a persistent user model and entity-aware context, cutting inference cost 40-60 % via a six-tier token budget. Mimosa moves beyond fixed workflows with an evolving multi-agent framework that auto-synthesizes task-specific topologies, executes via code-generating agents, and scores itself with an LLM judge; on ScienceAgentBench it reaches 43.1 % success, beating both single-agent and static multi-agent baselines. InternAgent-1.5 unifies generation, verification, and evolution subsystems to run end-to-end scientific discovery across computational and wet-lab domains, topping GAIA, HLE and FrontierScience benchmarks. Prompt engineering is also shifting: RASPRef shows prompts themselves can be optimized without human labels by retrieving past trajectories and using verifier feedback to iteratively refine the prompt [TRUNCATED]

uap.findings: Congressional UAP hearings continue into 2026, with Rep. Anna Paulina Luna leading House Oversight investigations into non-disclosure of UAP data. A September 2025 hearing featured whistleblowers alleging cover-ups, videos of orbs deflecting missiles, and demands for transparency from the Pentagon's AARO. The Task Force on Declassification of Federal Secrets is actively requesting classified UAP footage and documents, signaling sustained legislative pressure for disclosure of potential non-human intelligence encounters.

weather: Today in Edmonds: Cloudy morning giving way to partly sunny afternoon, high 60-62°F. Light SW winds 5-10 mph. No rain expected. Clear to partly cloudy tonight, low around 48-51°F. Pleasant spring day—perfect for a walk along the waterfront.

local_news.findings: Edmonds City Council postponed until next week its vote on confirming Stephanie Lucash as the new city administrator. The council also advanced a measure to clarify what happens when a council meeting falls on an election day, and Mayor Mike Rosen broke a 3-3 tie to send a proposal to 'unpause' the city's volunteer salary commission to next week's consent agenda. Planning Director Mike Clugston reported that the city issued 15 permits for detached accessory dwelling units (DADUs) last year and is developing a pre-approved plan program to streamline the process. Major development projects nearing approval include the 411-unit Edmonds Green and the 258-unit Apollo Apartments.

The City of Edmonds issued an update on ongoing efforts to resolve operational issues at the Edmonds Wastewater Treatment Plant. The carbon recovery system, intended to replace the aging incinerator with a more sustainable gasification system, has experienced significant delays and technical failures since construction began in 2021. The project cost has escalated from $26 million to $31 million, and the city has spent over $5 million hauling wastewater solids to a landfill in eastern Oregon. On April 14, the council approved a $415,000 contract with Jacobs Engineering to serve as an independent consultant to ensure the city can operate the facility once substantial completion is reached.

The Edmonds City Council unanimously adopted the city's first-ever Comprehensive Safety Action Plan, which identifies 11 priority traffic projects citywide that could be eligible for future grant funding. The plan, funded through a U.S. Department of Transportation Safe Streets and Roads for All Grant, serves as a roadmap to identify priority locations where crashes and near misses happen often or are most severe. The 11 projects include improvements to signalized intersections, downtown core unsignalized intersections, and several Highway 99 corridors. Police Chief Loi Dawkins reported that the department is working to meet state requirements to access public safety sales tax dollars that Edmonds has been collecting since February.

global_news.findings: US and Iranian forces exchanged fire in the Strait of Hormuz, with the US military saying it intercepted "unprovoked Iranian attacks" and responded with "self-defence strikes" as three guided-missile destroyers transited the waterway. Iranian state media reported explosions near Bandar Abbas and Qeshm Island, describing an "exchange of fire" between Iranian forces and "the enemy." President Trump called the incident a "love tap" and insisted the ceasefire remains intact, while urging Iran to accept a 14-point memorandum of understanding to end the war.

Indonesia's Mount Dukono volcano erupted, killing three hikers including two foreigners and one local resident. The volcano, located on Halmahera island, sent huge plumes of ash into the sky. Mount Dukono is one of Indonesia's most active volcanoes, and the eruption prompted evacuation efforts in the surrounding areas.

The United States is awaiting what it hopes will be a "serious offer" from Iran in response to Washington's latest proposal to end the war, despite the recent exchange of strikes between the two nations. The diplomatic efforts continue amid heightened tensions following the Strait of Hormuz incident, with both sides maintaining their positions while engaging in back-channel negotiations.

New suspected hantavirus cases have been identified in Spain and on the remote island of Tristan da Cunha, expanding the outbreak beyond the cruise ship cluster that has already killed three passengers. Health officials are racing to track the spread of the virus while urging calm, emphasizing that this is not the next pandemic. The World Health Organization has convened emergency meetings to coordinate the international response.

The ongoing Iran war has cost Toyota approximately £3 billion as soaring material prices and falling sales take their toll on the global automotive industry. The conflict has disrupted supply chains and increased costs for manufacturers worldwide, with particular impact on companies dependent on Middle Eastern oil and materials. The economic ripple effects are being felt across multiple sectors as the war continues to impact global trade.

intellectual_journals.findings: Sean Irving argues that economic republicanism—19th-century heirs to Machiavelli and Jefferson—offers a forgotten blueprint for freedom today. The core idea is that liberty is not just the absence of interference but the absence of domination: citizens must wield counter-power in both politics and the economy. Irving traces how British trade-unionists, 1848 revolutionaries, and American land-reformers translated the republican fear of arbitrary rule into fights against corporate monopolies and wage dependence. The essay’s contemporary payoff is a call to revive "economic constitutionalism": pu [TRUNCATED]

Flora Champy revisits Marcel Proust’s 1905 essay "On Reading" to defend literature as a technology of freedom rather than moral improvement. Proust rejects Ruskin’s utilitarian claim that books make us better; instead, he argues that reading is an intimate, disinterested encounter that lets another mind inhabit us. By suspending the cost-benefit calculus of social life, literature rehearses the act of choosing who we become. Champy extends the point to the algorithmic age: if big data profiles us for advertisers, the slow, solitary practice of reading reclaims authorship of the self.

Denva Gallant examines 13th-century mosaics in Venice’s San Marco to show how medieval art turned the body into a political text. In the Zen Chapel vault, Saint Mark is dragged by executioners yet remains impassive; his composure is not mere hagiography but a visual argument that spiritual authority transcends physical coercion. Gallant highlights the racialized figure of the Black executioner, whose exaggerated musculature and violence naturalize hierarchy. Repeated across centuries, such images trained viewers to read social order as bodily destiny—a lesson, Gallant warns, that modern visual [TRUNCATED]

Tim Judah reports from Kyiv on Ukraine’s third winter of full-scale war. Rolling blackouts and nightly Russian drone strikes have turned survival into a civic routine: cafes brew coffee on propane, theaters rehearse by candlelight, and families time their showers to the thud of distant explosions. Yet Judah finds a paradoxical calm—Andrey Kurkov tells him, "the future is just too far away; we live every day only in reality." The piece captures how a society adapts when the horizon collapses into the next dawn, and how the mundane—potatoes, jam, a warm radiator—becomes the texture of resistance [TRUNCATED]

Maria Popova distills Oliver Sacks’s posthumous essay collection to a single insight: perception is not a passive window but a creative mirror. Drawing on neurological patients who experience time as stuttering frames, Sacks argues that consciousness is a coalition-making process—neurons must recruit allies across the brain to push any moment above the threshold of awareness. This means that what we notice is less a record of the world than a portrait of who we are. Popova links the finding to Proust: just as memory edits the past, perception edits the present, and both crafts are acts of self [TRUNCATED]

wearable_ai.findings: Voice-first AI hardware has expanded beyond the familiar Friend pendant to include Audria, a discreet behind-the-ear device that proactively listens to conversations and provides context-aware assistance. Audria, founded in 2025 and employing 7 people across Delaware and India, positions itself as "personal computing for everyone" with a mission to reach even villages in developing countries. The  [TRUNCATED]

The specialized AI tool landscape for English and History teachers has matured significantly with three distinct platforms. Humy.ai (founded 2023, 8 employees) now offers 1,200+ interactive AI-powered historical figures plus 90+ teacher tools, serving 60,000+ teachers with auto-graded assignments and differentiated reading levels. Hello History provides similar historical figure interactions but f [TRUNCATED]

The lifelogging wearable category has evolved into sophisticated AI jewelry with Nirva leading as an "AI jewelry that journals your life" - a beautiful, all-day wearable that builds a "living library of your life" as the memory layer for personal AI ecosystems. Nirva, which won 6 awards at CES 2026, represents the jewelry-grade end of the spectrum. PIN AI offers an open platform approach, democrat [TRUNCATED]

newyorker.title: In HBO’s “The Dark Wizard,” Dean Potter Climbs On
newyorker.dek: The BASE jumper died in a tragic accident in 2015, the day before he was to accept an award for performance art. A new docuseries explores his life and legacy. Nick Paumgarten writes.
newyorker.text:
The morning before Dean Potter died, in May, 2015, he and his girlfriend, Jennifer Rapp, were puttering around their house in Yosemite. Potter, the world-famous rock climber and daredevil, had just returned from a trip to Germany, and Rapp had flown in from Vancouver, the headquarters of the apparel company Arc’teryx, where she was head of communications. In the living room, Rapp tried on some dresses for Potter. “Help me pick one,” she said.
They were scheduled to fly the next morning to New York, to attend a gala where he was to receive something called an Action Maverick Award. Potter disliked New York, and cities in general, and planned on staying just one night, but he was uncommonly excited about this particular honor, because it recognized him as an artist. Recently, he’d been trying, mostly in vain, to convince the world, and himself, that he performed vertiginous feats—free soloing on El Capitan, highlining over Yosemite Falls, BASE jumping in a wingsuit off the Eiger—not to stoke his ego or his reputation but, instead, to express an elegant swashbuckling concord with the natural world. Having been surpassed, as an athlete, by Alex Honnold, the new free soloist in the valley, Potter, then in his early forties, reimagined himself as a performance artist, of a kind. The Maverick Award was a validation.
Potter helped Rapp choose a dress: one by Missoni, with bright colors. A friend, Graham Hunt, Potter’s wingsuiting sidekick, was due to drop by to do tree work on land they’d bought nearby, but, preferring to chill out alone with Rapp, Potter left a message telling him not to come. Hunt didn’t get that message, though, and turned up soon afterward. “That changed the course of our day,” Rapp said recently. “We wouldn’t have hung out with Graham, and Dean wouldn’t have died.” Not on that day, anyway.
That afternoon, Potter, Rapp, and Hunt hiked out to Taft Point, a prow of granite overlooking Yosemite Valley. In their flying-squirrel suits, Hunt and Potter jumped off a promontory and swooped toward a ridge with a slot in it called the Notch. They didn’t make it. Rapp, up on Taft Point, heard two thuds. A recovery team found the bodies in the Notch the next day.
The accident in the Notch is the climax of “The Dark Wizard,” a new four-part HBO documentary about Potter and his demons. The film’s directors, Nick Rosen and Peter Mortimer, and its co-creator Josh Lowell, also made “The Alpinist,” in 2021, about another doomed mountain man, and they produce Reel Rock, a perennial travelling festival of climbing films, so they’re accustomed to tragic endings. Still, the latest film, owing to Potter’s emotional turbulence and some of the wreckage he left behind, was the most fraught project they’ve taken on.
A few days after Potter died, Rapp received an e-mail from the woman behind the Maverick Awards, a choreographer named Elizabeth Streb. Potter and Rapp had first met her at the Telluride Mountainfilm Festival, where she was promoting Catherine Gund’s movie about her, “Born to Fly: Elizabeth Streb vs. Gravity.” Streb’s work incorporates what she called “extreme action,” a lot of falling and crashing into things. She and Potter hit it off.
“He, like me, believed he could fly, and dedicated his life to that proposition,” Streb said the other day. “We have a theory of the real move—it’s one that once initiated you can’t stop. We specialize in letting the ground stop us and training our bodies to take the hit. There’s no such thing as perfect safety. There is only preparing and training.”
The night after Potter died, Streb, at her gala, presented his award in absentia. “I am gathering everything together for you,” she wrote afterward, in her e-mail to Rapp. “The Award itself, with his name and an inscription”; also, “a video we put together of his work [and] a video of an ‘Action’ my dancers performed for him called ‘Head First’ and a few photos of the Evening.”
Rapp, in her desolation, never replied, so she never got to see the award or the videos. They haven’t spoken since. Rapp doesn’t have the dress anymore. ♦

literary_pick.title: The Brief Wondrous Life of Oscar Wao
literary_pick.author: Junot Díaz
literary_pick.summary: Winner of the 2008 Pulitzer Prize and the National Book Critics Circle Award, this novel follows Oscar de León, a sweet but overweight Dominican-American nerd from New Jersey who dreams of becoming the Dominican Tolkien and finding love. The story spans generations, tracing the fukú—a curse that haunts Oscar’s family from Santo Domingo to the U.S.—and explores themes of identity, love, colonialism, and the immigrant experience. Blending Dominican history, pop culture, and magical realism, the novel has been hailed as a landmark work of 21st-century American fiction and a future classic.
